Likely due to the small size of the internal storage, the previous owner had only done a very minimal install of what we can now confirm was Debian 6; many pieces were missing or didn't work properly.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiyDy_tZZDp_8FZP2jc-3LvNCYua7UgGZOU6pjPr87ZpxLGix3_-UcTRUQE0BYgS6XmpxcZZ95XNIgn1zDIUXiUIZHHmn7YEYfpb2mahCwBQwaodNdR7LKMa9YvYORtO_O1PfhtD5HWqh3WUS_CfitjO2fuLTG5IDFppKOhAUxnWNfqITFERLLgUpNQges/s320/vlcsnap-2026-04-24-23h31m23s607.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiyDy_tZZDp_8FZP2jc-3LvNCYua7UgGZOU6pjPr87ZpxLGix3_-UcTRUQE0BYgS6XmpxcZZ95XNIgn1zDIUXiUIZHHmn7YEYfpb2mahCwBQwaodNdR7LKMa9YvYORtO_O1PfhtD5HWqh3WUS_CfitjO2fuLTG5IDFppKOhAUxnWNfqITFERLLgUpNQges/s1620/vlcsnap-2026-04-24-23h31m23s607.png.jpg)
Still, we could see (fuzzily) that we were using a 2.6 kernel and that the CPU was detected as a "Loongson-2" on a `lemote-yeeloong-2f-8.9inches`. I noted the BogoMIPS of 522.24. How much performance could we expect from this machine?
Interestingly, "someone" (probably ICT) had already posted [a CoreMark score](https://www.eembc.org/viewer/?benchmark_seq=1174) of 1813.1 iterations/sec and a CoreMark/MHz of 2.2664. I suspect this was ICT/BLX themselves because it was run on an original 800MHz STLS2F01 but posted rather late in October 2010. This rating was obtained with `gcc` 4.4.2 and `-march=loongson2f -O3` on Linux 2.6.32, nearly exactly the setup we have here, so I tried duplicating it myself with CoreMark 1.01 (using `gcc` 4.4.5, modifying CoreMark to use the same optimization settings and selecting the best score):
``` CoreMark Size : 666 Total ticks : 16867 Total time (secs): 16.867000 Iterations/Sec : 1778.620976 Iterations : 30000 Compiler version : GCC4.4.5 Compiler flags : -march=loongson2f -O3 -DPERFORMANCE_RUN=1 -lrt
```
That's pretty close, within about two percent, and yielding a CoreMark/MHz of 2.234. The `-march=loongson2f` does appear to be necessary for best performance; without it CoreMark reported a best score of 1652.619402, about seven percent slower.
But don't miss the forest for the trees — these are not great numbers even compared with older contemporaries. For example, my 2005 Mac mini NetBSD router, with a 1.5GHz PowerPC G4 7447A and 512K of L2, at the default _`-O2`_ and _no_ architectural optimization gets 6073.992269 and a CoreMark/MHz of 4.049. Cranked up to `-O3 -mcpu=7450`, it pulls down a score of 6544.892009 and a CoreMark/MHz of 4.363. Even considering the Yeeloong's ultra-low-end market placement, it's not a good look to be beaten so thoroughly by a low-end desktop that's three years older.
The real heck of it is when you compare to the earlier Godson-1B. At 250MHz it manages a [CoreMark of 670.36](https://www.eembc.org/viewer/?benchmark_seq=1304), or a CoreMark/MHz of ... 2.6815. And the worst part is this isn't the worst part, and to get to the worst part, let's tell a bit more of the story before we try running OpenBSD.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgK80zwLVDARL-oU5OT8uK53DLxSHOux-tKdoRy5KjmFvWm0Dz5Dvb-KFs9b8WqhXE6M8iQExsyX0aHeoxOHfAkoiylui_nwEalHMyFgBJC2i59pq1kk8WcZA_k2yCiMYq9zAMuW9ss4yUmDmxBZKseXrDY-xS9tKoREA4sXA7nzWqqmVTbb8dEnTJw8l8/s320/gdium.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgK80zwLVDARL-oU5OT8uK53DLxSHOux-tKdoRy5KjmFvWm0Dz5Dvb-KFs9b8WqhXE6M8iQExsyX0aHeoxOHfAkoiylui_nwEalHMyFgBJC2i59pq1kk8WcZA_k2yCiMYq9zAMuW9ss4yUmDmxBZKseXrDY-xS9tKoREA4sXA7nzWqqmVTbb8dEnTJw8l8/s2400/gdium.jpg)
Although the Yeeloong was the first Loongson laptop, it wasn't long before there was another. STMicroelectronics was a well-known company that had much better yield and greater fab capacity at the time than SMIC did, making the chip both cheap enough and available enough to interest other low-end OEMs, and one machine that actually reached market was the notorious [EMTEC Gdium Liberty 1000](web.archive.org/web/20090417173501/http://www.gdium.com/en/product) subnotebook. EMTEC was the remnant of BASF Magnetics, BASF's magnetic tape subsidiary, which was spun off in 1991 and sold (acquiring its new name) in 1997. By 2006, after passing through multiple hands and the divestment of its tape business and related IP, it was bought by Dexxon Group, a French computer products company.
The Gdium was EMTEC's first, and eventually only, computer. It was very similar to the 8101B with its own webcam and a similar 10.1" 1024x600 LCD, but ran the STLS2F02 at 900MHz with 512MB or 1GB of RAM, used a different audio codec and a slightly later but compatible SiliconMotion SM502 with 16MB of VRAM for 1280x1024 VGA output, and instead of a hard disk it dedicated one of the three USB ports to a special "G-Key" (a USB flash drive in a unique form factor) for primary storage. The included G-Key was preloaded with G-Linux, a modified version of Mandriva Linux, and came in sizes up to 32GB.
Although slated to emerge in September 2008, possibly even beating the Yeeloong, manufacturing delays held the Gdium back until February 2009 when it was introduced at €379 ($485 at spot, or about $750 in 2026 dollars). Overly expensive to begin with, the base price States-side was quickly slashed to $399 \[$620\] and then $349 \[$540\], but reviewers soured on the frustrating trackpad, poor battery life, middling performance and startling levels of heat (measuring 112 degrees Fahrenheit on the bottom in [one review](https://www.laptopmag.com/reviews/desktops/emtec-gdium-liberty-1000)). Perhaps its most interesting aspect was the [One Laptop Per Hacker](https://web.archive.org/web/20090311024841/http://olph.gdium.com/) seed program (obviously based on One Laptop Per Child) to foster greater developer interest in MIPS and Loongson, but the idea failed as resoundingly as the computer did, and Gdium sales ended around 2010.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhjshl3qqfESW3U6ekHu4NfHQ4_s7k-wLFb7bUfXx0x8kBxTxGE_Q96dcNFEyvkJswt8q_pSTkdSuTNvsGfsFdd15DdiS8unUTjFlDF8cqCxp2ifl0lr7wYRGJxdSY2FZxgnXVqv3V3cJyfiqL5xnxu5ybj5honoRQ0VAqP28ALPPg-RaPFYpN_bdowJh4/s320/jisus.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhjshl3qqfESW3U6ekHu4NfHQ4_s7k-wLFb7bUfXx0x8kBxTxGE_Q96dcNFEyvkJswt8q_pSTkdSuTNvsGfsFdd15DdiS8unUTjFlDF8cqCxp2ifl0lr7wYRGJxdSY2FZxgnXVqv3V3cJyfiqL5xnxu5ybj5honoRQ0VAqP28ALPPg-RaPFYpN_bdowJh4/s1600/jisus.jpg)
Another planned Loongson-2F laptop, the suspiciously-named [Jisus](https://web.archive.org/web/20080408213413/http://vanderled.com/onlinestore/product_info.php/cPath/49_48_58/products_id/81) (pronounced just like _that_ guy?) from Dutch integrator Van Der Led, promised to run the processor at the full 1GHz but inside a smaller shell like the 8089. In fact, the system specs showed it to be a near total clone of the 8089A except for a 4GB flash drive and an even lower resolution 8.9" screen at 800x480, plus your choice of prettier cases so you can admire its colour as the heat melts the plastic before your very eyes. This wouldn't have been terrible for €299, but not days after the initial April 2008 announcement the company announced the x86-based Jisus 2 instead, and then the Jisus 3, and then [never released any of them](https://liliputing.com/jisus-netbook-update/). In this case, this Jisus never had a _first_ coming, let alone a second. I'll get my coat.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjmi4itr-UVhyphenhyphenIrJ1dJU2mzkxr-lqGyaQfdQ2o3i_lp9tyDeiUHG-5Yq5P9OjJHR-q8gIwra0vJNx3y7MQbbF4Jb53hHHqnHqvHSuK0VgRA9frBe5oQuE_xPdD3w2rS-EOiigtMV1NpS5VcMjNE5-6RxIPDhSPnsfOULD19lyufvG2FiiWbY6bwFQ43zn4/s320/godson-3.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjmi4itr-UVhyphenhyphenIrJ1dJU2mzkxr-lqGyaQfdQ2o3i_lp9tyDeiUHG-5Yq5P9OjJHR-q8gIwra0vJNx3y7MQbbF4Jb53hHHqnHqvHSuK0VgRA9frBe5oQuE_xPdD3w2rS-EOiigtMV1NpS5VcMjNE5-6RxIPDhSPnsfOULD19lyufvG2FiiWbY6bwFQ43zn4/s3000/godson-3.png)
In 2008 ICT began work on the multicore Godson-3, starting with four GS464 cores on a crossbar, plus specialized instructions for faster x86 emulation; that same year, ICT and their own spin-off BLX also established Loongson Technology Corporation Limited as a public-private partnership to further commercialize the architecture. Meanwhile, ICT and MIPS Technologies finally put aside their differences completely and ICT became a direct licensee of the MIPS32 and MIPS64 architectures in 2009 (with LTC following in 2011).
These positive developments started to make the architecture attractive to other operating systems as a new modern MIPS implementation, and early work began in August 2009 on an OpenBSD port starting with the Gdium (NetBSD didn't add support for Loongson CPUs until 2012 with 6.0, and specific support for the Yeeloong with 7.0 in 2015). Unfortunately, the corners Lemote had cut with their PMON port made the booter and installer portions more complicated, and by November a new and critical problem emerged: while the systems could now install and boot, they would then repeatedly freeze, sometimes while appearing to do nothing at all.
It turns out two serious hardware bugs could trigger when the Loongson-2F incorrectly predicts a branch, though the errata initially only appeared in Chinese until a discussion explaining the issue turned up in November on the [`binutils`](https://sourceware.org/legacy-ml/binutils/2009-11/msg00387.html) list. Recall that MIPS processors have a delay slot after a branch, an unfortunate holdover from early implementations, which is not always but often filled with a `nop` instruction. Like all delay slots, the instruction in it is always executed whether or not the branch is taken. The canonical MIPS `nop` is `sll $0,$0,0` (shift the hardwired zero register left by zero bits), encoded as 0x00000000.
The first problem was fortunately relatively easy to work around. Loongson-2F has an eight-entry internal branch queue. A branch queue entry is generated when a branch instruction goes into the reorder queue, at most one per cycle. After the branch instruction is executed, the queue entry records the target program counter (for register jumps), the branch direction (for conditional branches), and whether the prediction was erroneous, all of which are used by its other branch prediction support hardware. When a branch is mispredicted, it is marked as "in error" and information from its queue entry is used to cancel instructions in the pipeline downstream of the faulty prediction, including any renamed registers as tracked in the physical register mapping table (PRMT). Entries are evicted from the branch queue as new branches are encountered or mispredicts occur. The bug manifests if the queue is _full_ when a mispredict occurs. ICT/BLX's mitigation for this was to change how the assembler renders `nop`: instead of `sll $0,$0,0`, it uses `or $at,$at,$0`, which is practically a no-op because it logical-ORs register $at with the zero register (i.e., result is $at), and then stores that in $at. MIPS and other RISCs use the same or similar notion for register moves generally. The post notes this isn't a complete fix, but the 2F's prediction accuracy is fairly good to begin with, and with this change the bug apparently becomes so rare as to be practically nonexistent.
The post doesn't say _why_ this works, but we can — wait for it — _speculate_ from the STMicroelectronics STLS2F01 user manual (which mercifully _is_ written in English). When the triggering mispredict happens on a full queue, the processor might access the wrong branch queue entry and thus improperly free or fail to free allocated resources, potentially making insufficient registers available for renaming in future speculation. The processor can't recover if that happens. However, the manual implies there's something special about how the cancellation process handles the instruction in the delay slot (the sentence "Delay slot instructions should be paid special attention in branch canceling" on page 21), because this instruction must always be executed even in a mispredict. The instruction is usually `nop`, and since the canonical `nop` only references the hardwired zero register, no renaming is needed because its value is invariant. But, by replacing every canonical `nop` with `or $at,$at,$0`, _now_ at least one mapping will need to be made in the PRMT, and since the delay slot is guaranteed to execute, at least one register can be recovered when this instruction is retired while unwinding the mispredict. A different instruction in the delay slot would generally behave likewise. Assuming I'm right, which is always a good assumption, that appears to be just enough to let the processor right itself in the vast majority of cases.
Such a tweak by itself would have had a negligible impact on performance, but the second flaw is more pernicious. The Loongson-2F does not cancel memory reads occurring as a consequence of a mispredict, even though it will restore any affected registers, based on the expectation the memory will likely be used later anyway and we might as well fill the cache line now. Although doing so would ordinarily be an idempotent operation, some external chipsets will act or fault on such a read, speculative or otherwise — and unfortunately one of those chips is the AMD CS5536 in the Fuloong and Yeeloong, where particular address ranges will cause it to hang. Because a speculative read can theoretically occur of any address, including addresses in the 16-entry branch target buffer, the fault can lock up any machine with vulnerable hardware.
User code is not affected by this because the faulty access will trigger a TLB miss, causing an exception and aborting the prediction. The kernel, however, is not so constrained and can crash readily, most notoriously from a poison leftover branch target buffer entry that originated in user code. The workaround (painfully) is another assembler feature to modify all jumps in the kernel and patch their destination address to avoid the poison address ranges, which, if the destination is in a register, will require inserting additional instructions to modify the target. Doing so will foul the branch prediction cache, but the kernel is also explicitly made to invalidate it anyway crossing a kernel-userland boundary, so the bug can't happen. This somewhat nauseating fix wallpapered what were occasional Linux crashes at the cost of kernel performance and is present in our Debian 6 kernel, but the OpenBSD port was much more severely afflicted to the point of unusability until [Miod Vallat discovered the errata post](https://undeadly.org/cgi?action=article&sid=20100225062850).
OpenBSD 4.7 thus introduced support for Loongson-2E and -2F in November 2010, becoming the first BSD to successfully run on the Gdium, Fuloong, Yeeloong, and Lynloong all-in-one (basically a Fuloong strapped to a 1366x768 LCD). While ICT and STMicroelectronics corrected both errata for what was termed the Loongson-2G, first by fixing the branch queue problem and second by eliminating the spurious read, the -2G appears as a -2F to running software and it's currently impossible to know which one your machine has without cracking it open (which we're not going to do for this article, no offense). When OpenBSD [moved from `gcc` to `clang`](https://www.cambus.net/the-state-of-toolchains-in-openbsd/) due to licensing changes, similar changes [had to be made to LLVM](https://bugs.llvm.org/show_bug.cgi?id=49611), delaying the migration on Loongson until December 2020. This will become relevant when we look at system performance.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiLSnpaVJ7jhOLeCerjfYRctLjDCsiMbZd2IT1J2-3JeSLD0NtM9hfw-HWqVyC1AeHD3o87D7TVrskQhaQz8d21-3Lk5BKdbr8o8wiDPLZuJjMzO1AApvESxiik0IawxMaLnJHpoBWfRJdNJJv1I0rNUVPWDqoxzWIp9riUD4AMK57EIL869EhFmneipjk/s320/PXL_20260622_034939703.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiLSnpaVJ7jhOLeCerjfYRctLjDCsiMbZd2IT1J2-3JeSLD0NtM9hfw-HWqVyC1AeHD3o87D7TVrskQhaQz8d21-3Lk5BKdbr8o8wiDPLZuJjMzO1AApvESxiik0IawxMaLnJHpoBWfRJdNJJv1I0rNUVPWDqoxzWIp9riUD4AMK57EIL869EhFmneipjk/s4080/PXL_20260622_034939703.jpg)
Now, where will we put our second operating system? Remember I said that Lemote PMON will only boot from an ext2 (or ext3) filesystem. On the other hand, the OpenBSD booter that loads the kernel will _not_ do so from a Linux filesystem because it has no disklabel. The standard way to install OpenBSD on Lemote-derived hardware, then, is to have a small ext2 partition that only contains the OpenBSD booter, and then the booter will load the OpenBSD kernel from the main partition, and Bob's your malignant spirit in Twin Peaks.
The problem we have is our pathetically small 2GB main drive and I'd like to keep the Debian that's already on it anyway for comparison purposes. That's fine; it's perfectly acceptable to boot from a USB stick, and this is also supported in Lemote PMON. But with only two USB ports on this basic 8089A (note from the future: we'll end up needing one of those ports very soon for something else) we should be able to boot from that nice internal USB card reader, which I verified does work in OpenBSD once you get the kernel up. A 32GB card would be plenty of space for anything we want to do in OpenBSD.
The trick is to not involve the booter at all — PMON is perfectly capable of loading the OpenBSD kernel directly, as long as it's on a filesystem it can access. At this point the OpenBSD fiends reading this have collapsed from apoplexy because the booter does other things like pass entropy. However, at least with current versions, we don't need those particular things to actually start the kernel; it might complain, but it will load. With that in mind, our plan is to let the install run to the SD card, which the OpenBSD installer can see, then copy the kernel to the 2GB internal drive and set the Yeeloong to boot from that. With the kernel up, we can then point the root device back to the main filesystem on the card, finish the boot, and once again Bob's your killer of Laura Palmer.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiDBTO6e1XECkAC2ott6se3PcLszRd2HJC0UgbdkzRJUVIWU6Y0u6M-p8eqCuIRZeVdfHI5ucD-8mZPdXe8ZGO8hp3DEsHUrGvahvjFwvDMQ29_-9BveAIoeluS9hCVawDU-Kxq93VNy9jFgVc9IbCZ5MgxsK32MNjswYj7RI1X6wf9qn_dZoJX1IH1VJc/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h57m54s417.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiDBTO6e1XECkAC2ott6se3PcLszRd2HJC0UgbdkzRJUVIWU6Y0u6M-p8eqCuIRZeVdfHI5ucD-8mZPdXe8ZGO8hp3DEsHUrGvahvjFwvDMQ29_-9BveAIoeluS9hCVawDU-Kxq93VNy9jFgVc9IbCZ5MgxsK32MNjswYj7RI1X6wf9qn_dZoJX1IH1VJc/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h57m54s417.png.jpg)
At the time I embarked upon this project OpenBSD 7.8 was still most current, so from the Linux side I put a copy of its BSD installer (which contains its own RAM disk image) in the root of the ext3 filesystem. `boot -k` is required to start this image or the installer kernel will be unable to identify the system type.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgOZpzFoxS-Fyaa-vDAsGOYM7eEP0YKqQPxq0_PlgQ2VES0HI_0OV126Ap7dbywNr3iKfdnsyblJ1G1mC_sywUdt3HL-hehogmN8YMbDQHH8QaFuwhvlD17lTHM5WlA1E9CjfS0TnC1iuu-HuY6q0mQjTdVdoSjExeeHDcGeWCWhVmQoL85Btxrg6pqr60/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h57m57s306.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgOZpzFoxS-Fyaa-vDAsGOYM7eEP0YKqQPxq0_PlgQ2VES0HI_0OV126Ap7dbywNr3iKfdnsyblJ1G1mC_sywUdt3HL-hehogmN8YMbDQHH8QaFuwhvlD17lTHM5WlA1E9CjfS0TnC1iuu-HuY6q0mQjTdVdoSjExeeHDcGeWCWhVmQoL85Btxrg6pqr60/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h57m57s306.png.jpg)
Initial messages. The CPU clock speed is indeed 796MHz. The main PCI-X devices (the Realtek 8139, SiliconMotion SM712 LynxEM+ and AMD CS5536) are enumerated.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjKTcq1iEMzTSkh4j7zXqU2ys43kBmbplj4w1aiFrXatjfysXemi4MFN4B6cqcSmaEfc7qgFPLf5JLkX2AK8dOKBNMdXHCPld7L418l9YIL1E9Q845lMP4279G_v8emufIeqEBnw85MbQ3M8TKuRhfWEedCg7n3t11XVPpr2dqOEyWeFtq4D9wPzgtC-LM/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h58m10s935.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjKTcq1iEMzTSkh4j7zXqU2ys43kBmbplj4w1aiFrXatjfysXemi4MFN4B6cqcSmaEfc7qgFPLf5JLkX2AK8dOKBNMdXHCPld7L418l9YIL1E9Q845lMP4279G_v8emufIeqEBnw85MbQ3M8TKuRhfWEedCg7n3t11XVPpr2dqOEyWeFtq4D9wPzgtC-LM/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-18h58m10s935.png.jpg)
On the USB bus, our Realtek internal card reader and the Realtek 8187B Wi-Fi are then detected via EHCI. We are ready to begin installation.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg73FfERQz6m2xVgBgKFw1IY-U903JW6EQiLKTx32L1QfvnprvAlbX22LQLH0hS7mix9lBETvYk0e4Hsb541VY_wKvfe-UxoXLBdQzYp4jaVz5evHnFNaU8TetJMaJaHrQ32oaLyqP2luX_dATdtSZyZcXPIu_UD36EJNlfTCx_vr_0c9-v_mKbTbIF33M/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h21m02s354.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg73FfERQz6m2xVgBgKFw1IY-U903JW6EQiLKTx32L1QfvnprvAlbX22LQLH0hS7mix9lBETvYk0e4Hsb541VY_wKvfe-UxoXLBdQzYp4jaVz5evHnFNaU8TetJMaJaHrQ32oaLyqP2luX_dATdtSZyZcXPIu_UD36EJNlfTCx_vr_0c9-v_mKbTbIF33M/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h21m02s354.png.jpg)
However, when I tried to actually _use_ the Realtek Wi-Fi (detected as `urtw0`), we were suddenly _unready_ to begin installation and got a big fat kernel panic. To see if it was the hardware in the Yeeloong or the OpenBSD driver, I dug out a TRENDNet TEW-424UB WiFi dongle which uses the same driver (detected as `urtw1`), and it crashed the same way.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjhyapj9saM3Y04CqwDsD1WSLHc7qWS-0qZsQBz1sXRbKy_h-sy_GuabcGUOsRsjLr9bPN63Dp08UBfzfkKx1b8Pf7WVDlh2m5SSvV7RhZksmsFdO-Vb5MswLkEH1GQIJ3BjbakaKJRwMCWv6e0u6T85DJRdWV_i4mzsKWUsqd4N5jJYxsbf-Fw0VLkABI/s320/PXL_20260622_035016156.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjhyapj9saM3Y04CqwDsD1WSLHc7qWS-0qZsQBz1sXRbKy_h-sy_GuabcGUOsRsjLr9bPN63Dp08UBfzfkKx1b8Pf7WVDlh2m5SSvV7RhZksmsFdO-Vb5MswLkEH1GQIJ3BjbakaKJRwMCWv6e0u6T85DJRdWV_i4mzsKWUsqd4N5jJYxsbf-Fw0VLkABI/s4080/PXL_20260622_035016156.jpg)
I dug out a couple more dongles from the junk box, but those were also the same chipset. I was dithering over using a really long Ethernet cable until finally I found this Belkin F5D7050 "Wireless G USB Network Adapter" using a Ralink chipset (contains an RT2573 medium access controller/baseband processor and an RT2528 RF transceiver). With a little USB extender it sits nicely on top of the microSD card adapter instead of out like the proverbial sore thumb, and has an easy-to-see activity LED.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiCIVYrIEkster2hPTeWfArjkr1emLji7KPigdnHRgfJk2BbW7LDJ35srnqqvSg081srsUMrOwoczxfaupKfVJxdcYVBpPE1uorMHN1jsSrKDbxUmtTuV7HSMDCYKiF4vp3b6zY0I7qTdtAwnlckg75pYK98WbevGWrflTkQNnUTFhQxMmgjWu_t-w0Xxc/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h23m20s250.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiCIVYrIEkster2hPTeWfArjkr1emLji7KPigdnHRgfJk2BbW7LDJ35srnqqvSg081srsUMrOwoczxfaupKfVJxdcYVBpPE1uorMHN1jsSrKDbxUmtTuV7HSMDCYKiF4vp3b6zY0I7qTdtAwnlckg75pYK98WbevGWrflTkQNnUTFhQxMmgjWu_t-w0Xxc/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h23m20s250.png.jpg)
OpenBSD detects it as `rum0` ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgj3FoJ26Bzt_Lt-FSNUpVLEpKDzYTf2gdyYapHk6viSh0hzJhWW9AKKqe9sPw9_Yc0acaEfvL6pz_JZN_B7br7jIKvwyoCU1YTnSWXiOederEACpfbvzX3AEeBMU6YXSVWxrby_MuNkhDmHeZQ-P_8SwNe-QlCAfSJ8WhAs_n8D-ajCbJPWmGFL9VU7rA/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h25m07s021.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgj3FoJ26Bzt_Lt-FSNUpVLEpKDzYTf2gdyYapHk6viSh0hzJhWW9AKKqe9sPw9_Yc0acaEfvL6pz_JZN_B7br7jIKvwyoCU1YTnSWXiOederEACpfbvzX3AEeBMU6YXSVWxrby_MuNkhDmHeZQ-P_8SwNe-QlCAfSJ8WhAs_n8D-ajCbJPWmGFL9VU7rA/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h25m07s021.png.jpg)
... and successfully configured it. Aren't you glad we still have a port left over?
It is generally my practice with laptops to name them after dog names, and desktops after notable people in their development or culture. To Westerners, Richard Stallman would be the notable person most associated with the Lemote Yeeloong. However, I can find no evidence that Richard Stallman had/has a dog, or indeed any pet. So this machine will simply be named `rms`, though I did toy with naming it `godson`. There's still time to change my mind.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjmj5xGpanpLm88S2TeWiDr1RmBKFKKmv5fT5lVAKaumutihT6c1KODR5cmLvAEPDADzMS23cGUYIsPZTCeiF-ybOIqjZgS4WPgjPuUrIrpDPMwaYjryHaAFcBe50hJeuqqf48ONqRTCR-IYlgTQ41aJ1HaD8KFGk06_ZjDpDS9prdEi_lsEZEyAVUptoI/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h39m06s857.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjmj5xGpanpLm88S2TeWiDr1RmBKFKKmv5fT5lVAKaumutihT6c1KODR5cmLvAEPDADzMS23cGUYIsPZTCeiF-ybOIqjZgS4WPgjPuUrIrpDPMwaYjryHaAFcBe50hJeuqqf48ONqRTCR-IYlgTQ41aJ1HaD8KFGk06_ZjDpDS9prdEi_lsEZEyAVUptoI/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h39m06s857.png.jpg)
Now we need to format and configure the SD card, which is `sd0`. Since we've chosen this as our root device, the OpenBSD installer also believes this is our boot volume (it's not, that's `wd0`). Right now this card as shipped from the SanDisk elves is all FAT32. At the end of installation the installer will expect it to have two partitions, the OpenBSD root and a Linux ext2 boot partition, and copy the booter to the Linux partition. This is made for us if we let OpenBSD use the "whole disk," but we're going to edit the MBR to explicitly specify the size of the SD card's ext2 partition (which we don't use anyway) as extra space for wear leveling.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg7dPn3pdUF3XFWB1795qRIi1WLAscyjNHvkwScWXaoy8miVbz_nckNHAdx-FXxes9zfsREVgUtwUBiVfhYZVp4MFLQM_TtYk4aAFhvHPqqsuANrqwtlgQC5oJWBx4ysQ4U-ua_X4STv_ja_1vczWjRjmxlAbHvkOsH9CdpMjfq643J7GjAWqKFcDiS9hQ/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h41m04s136.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg7dPn3pdUF3XFWB1795qRIi1WLAscyjNHvkwScWXaoy8miVbz_nckNHAdx-FXxes9zfsREVgUtwUBiVfhYZVp4MFLQM_TtYk4aAFhvHPqqsuANrqwtlgQC5oJWBx4ysQ4U-ua_X4STv_ja_1vczWjRjmxlAbHvkOsH9CdpMjfq643J7GjAWqKFcDiS9hQ/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h41m04s136.png.jpg)
Conveniently we know the total number of sectors from the initial state of the card. We edit our first slice, giving it a partition ID of $a6 for OpenBSD and 90% of the sectors, then edit the second, give it a partition ID of $83 for the Linux partition, and allocate it the remainder.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8dOpKeC9I4WeOunphwPWCx0BT8mCov2S4ULxJ2gVZGAZZZ90m-88yq9umH7V3cT6l9VA1RDcl4zTKcnGk1xcR9Byemd5Yn0Pzf6WZk8wcB7cVuqOlDiFmgondEftmBDebDeHHODuUpEtFq1K20kREOeu5tXPnWP9brVwZ_KwgW7G2CZT-nVoR4MLs94E/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h43m12s467.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8dOpKeC9I4WeOunphwPWCx0BT8mCov2S4ULxJ2gVZGAZZZ90m-88yq9umH7V3cT6l9VA1RDcl4zTKcnGk1xcR9Byemd5Yn0Pzf6WZk8wcB7cVuqOlDiFmgondEftmBDebDeHHODuUpEtFq1K20kREOeu5tXPnWP9brVwZ_KwgW7G2CZT-nVoR4MLs94E/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-19h43m12s467.png.jpg)
We quit, writing out the new MBR, then move on to the disklabel to configure the BSD (sub)partitions. It has correctly found the ext2 partition and assigned it as `sd0i`, but I'd rather just put everything in `/` (i.e., `sd0a`) because I'm one of _those_ people.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjkLI8zq057Pv7jLVaQ883ui8gBkl0yRSbON1tP3AyGClQapho5e9idTUvp39UCSTESkrYMwxBwN84UfAkVYEPb_LQ1nnpDOQ5xx8c_fbcE-X5N2MSK5X-FKfmlQlVHbBZKb-kyByHVISZfutAsn-40A_0kRKILJYYi_h1Wf73Ex9MkkhqwYPBXIi4qDPg/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h01m34s906.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjkLI8zq057Pv7jLVaQ883ui8gBkl0yRSbON1tP3AyGClQapho5e9idTUvp39UCSTESkrYMwxBwN84UfAkVYEPb_LQ1nnpDOQ5xx8c_fbcE-X5N2MSK5X-FKfmlQlVHbBZKb-kyByHVISZfutAsn-40A_0kRKILJYYi_h1Wf73Ex9MkkhqwYPBXIi4qDPg/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h01m34s906.png.jpg)
Notice that `sd0c`, the "whole disk," actually ends where our ext2 partition begins.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgs9VtT65o8EvZoJUr3KkAaFB0XeKHse0BLInUc9UpQVtnzb6ZzGDcVW1kri8Ll5b1ZfsbapGBRv7dZ4VtZEEhC6jjS5MsYQRoOWHs5O3_nc3eLfuAGMS5dz55CyMx_sZhRoicIZrQNwT9LFmjFkjtjcJMKEQa6dJpxgZPCSSn5emIl8x7n7ulU9utPGy8/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h08m06s431.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgs9VtT65o8EvZoJUr3KkAaFB0XeKHse0BLInUc9UpQVtnzb6ZzGDcVW1kri8Ll5b1ZfsbapGBRv7dZ4VtZEEhC6jjS5MsYQRoOWHs5O3_nc3eLfuAGMS5dz55CyMx_sZhRoicIZrQNwT9LFmjFkjtjcJMKEQa6dJpxgZPCSSn5emIl8x7n7ulU9utPGy8/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h08m06s431.png.jpg)
We manually blow away everything but `/` and swap (and the Linux slice), then move the swap next to the Linux partition so that the root will hold the rest.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi01i6qYcYq-uLiSAj278mPvM0giZB-4u5xs3raXVR55Db4DL3O3S66QN-eA4UY1OPjzGy3yitojErwCA-iTuK83WFjt6AWo0qrmxNzdPJyGEN01tjhEJaBlSYyeDUUy5YtSMMg1D6Khw3st_mmPmB_iBZJAUpG6Yklpm1vO3LS3isIAKIK1K2zz80WwQo/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h13m37s408.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi01i6qYcYq-uLiSAj278mPvM0giZB-4u5xs3raXVR55Db4DL3O3S66QN-eA4UY1OPjzGy3yitojErwCA-iTuK83WFjt6AWo0qrmxNzdPJyGEN01tjhEJaBlSYyeDUUy5YtSMMg1D6Khw3st_mmPmB_iBZJAUpG6Yklpm1vO3LS3isIAKIK1K2zz80WwQo/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h13m37s408.png.jpg)
Finally, we write out the new disklabel and then proceed to getting our install packages. Since Floodgap Orbiting HQ is in sunny California, we will use [the mirror](https://mirrors.sonic.net/pub/OpenBSD/) provided by the kind folks at Sonic. I am not affiliated with them nor a subscriber, but it's nice of them to provide a mirror anyway.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJ3J-DxYELS5W52NM4zLTNwmv48Rlpzjj3qgXjl7zWAFoKxllRbipWc6PL9TgzKq2Nv0lQaFxGtCbkS7Y1CKvWk4AjwdPd5f7AYNT-k1p8TQ1aVNykRj9PsySyqb1ZznvwRNP2N0Tau5Iql498JwL_9usuFobdI5iGlo4-EIcc94RAzLRdXyIJfUXQyiU/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h54m18s256.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJ3J-DxYELS5W52NM4zLTNwmv48Rlpzjj3qgXjl7zWAFoKxllRbipWc6PL9TgzKq2Nv0lQaFxGtCbkS7Y1CKvWk4AjwdPd5f7AYNT-k1p8TQ1aVNykRj9PsySyqb1ZznvwRNP2N0Tau5Iql498JwL_9usuFobdI5iGlo4-EIcc94RAzLRdXyIJfUXQyiU/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-20h54m18s256.png.jpg)
The Belkin easily downloads the base installation sets ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgzbCNXAbTMYOQa7UeoUi9Oaj-CaQgvh_GCUyHx92suP3bJ41lUX4_ZCI0GEhuE3fxTSVyTzeRvw6fz_aFERubPjok-xNxrzkRNod2CRu5sVKKKJjpZMu1GE3TGp83xha18pZp3eZsgUX8kI0BlW0ZJ9nvZCEwkgqf_v4tblg3F61vk1dChf7JdHNuwdyc/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h07m06s733.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgzbCNXAbTMYOQa7UeoUi9Oaj-CaQgvh_GCUyHx92suP3bJ41lUX4_ZCI0GEhuE3fxTSVyTzeRvw6fz_aFERubPjok-xNxrzkRNod2CRu5sVKKKJjpZMu1GE3TGp83xha18pZp3eZsgUX8kI0BlW0ZJ9nvZCEwkgqf_v4tblg3F61vk1dChf7JdHNuwdyc/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h07m06s733.png.jpg)
... and writes them out to disk.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEganSWI2bz4YYH_G5ulVcx1051W2R0ixPKIaZ-cAm4lXCyKIiMSh6EVXCN4X1-0xr0_GjWHb6uwLrlzWh9u-NpsKx2BA-LvXIuSSR5_VhHFUysmf9r2VoG-mW-0mtHcF7f4Oz6Q-QH5EUwvGO-E-cOeC3S8B-bO7_M2hmTQ_StdrR6UaX6P8cKNrbjaBo0/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h11m15s951.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEganSWI2bz4YYH_G5ulVcx1051W2R0ixPKIaZ-cAm4lXCyKIiMSh6EVXCN4X1-0xr0_GjWHb6uwLrlzWh9u-NpsKx2BA-LvXIuSSR5_VhHFUysmf9r2VoG-mW-0mtHcF7f4Oz6Q-QH5EUwvGO-E-cOeC3S8B-bO7_M2hmTQ_StdrR6UaX6P8cKNrbjaBo0/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h11m15s951.png.jpg)
As the last step of the installer, it will create the Linux filesystem that we will likely never use again and put the booter in it that we will likely always ignore. But don't reboot yet!
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhsjn2oJ-X4vfgQ1fi9d4r1vQuvHOjZuqq2l_4Xw8-SYxBVElwMOnNSjGeEyZoCs0nIRHPISRINZxRFNAFCDhl1buiyQ_pQ-m88w_VNn8jbi4IkoiERplpGoHh0Xg6LHS0i0bVtUPvP_YmC3euROu9RjkEETOO_AZCoOqUO8WtHGaGov7IvsO-SHJtM3Ws/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m16s121.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhsjn2oJ-X4vfgQ1fi9d4r1vQuvHOjZuqq2l_4Xw8-SYxBVElwMOnNSjGeEyZoCs0nIRHPISRINZxRFNAFCDhl1buiyQ_pQ-m88w_VNn8jbi4IkoiERplpGoHh0Xg6LHS0i0bVtUPvP_YmC3euROu9RjkEETOO_AZCoOqUO8WtHGaGov7IvsO-SHJtM3Ws/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m16s121.png.jpg)
To make this boot the way _we_ want to, we'll now need to manually mount `/dev/wd0i` (that is, the Linux partition on the main storage) and put the newly installed kernel there. As you can see from the screenshot, this device is not automatically created since the installer technically is unaware of its existence, and the SD card is already on `/mnt`. Using `/mnt2` as a second mount point,
``` cd /dev sh MAKEDEV wd0 mount_ext2fs /dev/wd0i /mnt2 cp /mnt/bsd /mnt2 umount /mnt2
```
will do the copy. We then manually unmount the SD card ( `umount /mnt`) and reboot.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjsaT0qzIuNKO16P0RuO-O2ysmQkAn5ePrLg3HWI_osOF_1Bg-n5Va_VACqIUG1zkAZ2_bDygQobfHizM3IyLbe3Ych-9R24wb9jLzGGuYnP7TdRhmiBDz-gFI8sGQw2ZRx6ha8cHEG04ssEtA6VTRZnk6GgG3TQZLbY2jH4SHwir6u1PsZnjA2QC-tki0/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m49s918.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjsaT0qzIuNKO16P0RuO-O2ysmQkAn5ePrLg3HWI_osOF_1Bg-n5Va_VACqIUG1zkAZ2_bDygQobfHizM3IyLbe3Ych-9R24wb9jLzGGuYnP7TdRhmiBDz-gFI8sGQw2ZRx6ha8cHEG04ssEtA6VTRZnk6GgG3TQZLbY2jH4SHwir6u1PsZnjA2QC-tki0/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m49s918.png.jpg)
Now we're going to boot the new kernel directly. We don't need the `-k` option for this, and you can (briefly) see that the hardware is properly autodetected.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjjMEvYzY_vpzGNCNgz4jXL76uA4aDjsQ0cpv3oaWOyuEKBuhHhVD_H6XMv2WG-4K5dw7QsMmrqVsrHsfGkMOXc7lR8I7if8xBpmP3VYz7ORRzZ7Xc-fK0okFL41TaIMj8VSSelfltPsVw92Vl3hVtJkoZqKp56l1_VVhqkbo7BGQIYuy1nGjNyAFKfKa0/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-20h47m44s202.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjjMEvYzY_vpzGNCNgz4jXL76uA4aDjsQ0cpv3oaWOyuEKBuhHhVD_H6XMv2WG-4K5dw7QsMmrqVsrHsfGkMOXc7lR8I7if8xBpmP3VYz7ORRzZ7Xc-fK0okFL41TaIMj8VSSelfltPsVw92Vl3hVtJkoZqKp56l1_VVhqkbo7BGQIYuy1nGjNyAFKfKa0/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-20h47m44s202.png.jpg)
The kernel comes up in a bigger font clearly meant for larger screens. We'll deal with this a little later.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhCbnPskuM6wEBO6osDIedqfdaTAxdO0Lv0bHqP5htUQo462H3qTb1A064Wubj4Pu3feGAaX333LBOwkF4NFmGU3DfUQ4Ox1ynoJDSf_6NRdp34765p20p9ycLVf6b-yTTxK5SXhBo_EkguKRKaj32b_PFgKc9ob2cQ6Cpxk-sZwewKy37Pn97cRhcplVY/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m54s393.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhCbnPskuM6wEBO6osDIedqfdaTAxdO0Lv0bHqP5htUQo462H3qTb1A064Wubj4Pu3feGAaX333LBOwkF4NFmGU3DfUQ4Ox1ynoJDSf_6NRdp34765p20p9ycLVf6b-yTTxK5SXhBo_EkguKRKaj32b_PFgKc9ob2cQ6Cpxk-sZwewKy37Pn97cRhcplVY/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h14m54s393.png.jpg)
Initial messages.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjS0E8WvD9LdNaGlzd-bY8SsnodOe9ZrwTW3evllqGIn2CZzkM8D0btxt_Y_cdIdYcq_xF9J4okuZ3a1NeoBwHiDZPfKsBWSJVIC5tVzUGnHTbxJtFPcLickVRsOzBtVDS0UMnMacmTkYPa4pZOowDkHmcVw7PyJ2KWr9rJ6mhyphenhyphenMLUZclYOr9fMHjnDUj8/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h15m20s035.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjS0E8WvD9LdNaGlzd-bY8SsnodOe9ZrwTW3evllqGIn2CZzkM8D0btxt_Y_cdIdYcq_xF9J4okuZ3a1NeoBwHiDZPfKsBWSJVIC5tVzUGnHTbxJtFPcLickVRsOzBtVDS0UMnMacmTkYPa4pZOowDkHmcVw7PyJ2KWr9rJ6mhyphenhyphenMLUZclYOr9fMHjnDUj8/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h15m20s035.png.jpg)
Because we kickstarted the kernel directly and not through the booter, it doesn't know where it booted from and thus what the root device is. So we tell it. Or rather, we egregiously gaslight it and point to the SD card.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEglDIR2jGSmG8PZKfw5Y0jFAKIR9cgzO_ggdFt1hBx4u_ni4y8P868uDGMWtUyqgio5fbvy_ryovkRQ-rmNQffgitpUvXeedT8A0_bBZIxoKHnfOgDfIRoP7w09PaRmW866vUaQn3ulYleaZOTCEITXGN_ke3WocOM-dTR4C8mHav-NzBDouMD6RZa3YP8/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h17m56s013.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEglDIR2jGSmG8PZKfw5Y0jFAKIR9cgzO_ggdFt1hBx4u_ni4y8P868uDGMWtUyqgio5fbvy_ryovkRQ-rmNQffgitpUvXeedT8A0_bBZIxoKHnfOgDfIRoP7w09PaRmW866vUaQn3ulYleaZOTCEITXGN_ke3WocOM-dTR4C8mHav-NzBDouMD6RZa3YP8/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h17m56s013.png.jpg)
Various critical components are reordered for ASLR protection upon boot from a standard installation. That step is not very fast, especially on this class of machine. Despite not having good initial entropy, it will still make our host keys and do other various first time setup tasks.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjlSg83wVupNVZEKzDjfECjOTsrkjJq_C3fmPOaNLrZfKOohLO5PrdNFA3XDU3QqJARvcbPFK0-CqyiLu1aBECmnZDHflcBVlcaKNevTkV6SsegFfxrtNKwOCDQIbxezrn76NOyGv9K4opwgQ4JTJ1s6IQbeSURnzl-hl462UGv5Plr_JlYxBdcBSBCu3g/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h18m11s033.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjlSg83wVupNVZEKzDjfECjOTsrkjJq_C3fmPOaNLrZfKOohLO5PrdNFA3XDU3QqJARvcbPFK0-CqyiLu1aBECmnZDHflcBVlcaKNevTkV6SsegFfxrtNKwOCDQIbxezrn76NOyGv9K4opwgQ4JTJ1s6IQbeSURnzl-hl462UGv5Plr_JlYxBdcBSBCu3g/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h18m11s033.png.jpg)
We can now log in. The root password and an unprivileged user (me) were already set up during installation.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnVksli2BsrmfNRHfNhWCjQqq6cI2ytiazeGVEk5C1Fs2-98t6N1cwejwJv7fVSx-iUz7x9xaScAh0m3fpj81qF2BF3sUOe09XFFHagPnAXDG3q77NeTfR2ydkyeVQQmaUcnfonSYEkOMBGAVFxXqAM7g5pNIp9f7AKo2_PWe6Ynzlb9y6NrzNHvKdd30/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h18m23s335.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnVksli2BsrmfNRHfNhWCjQqq6cI2ytiazeGVEk5C1Fs2-98t6N1cwejwJv7fVSx-iUz7x9xaScAh0m3fpj81qF2BF3sUOe09XFFHagPnAXDG3q77NeTfR2ydkyeVQQmaUcnfonSYEkOMBGAVFxXqAM7g5pNIp9f7AKo2_PWe6Ynzlb9y6NrzNHvKdd30/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h18m23s335.png.jpg)
We were instructed by the installer to read our E-mail on arrival, and here we have a message from Theo!
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhqI9Ovi4rYpx_MzlAVvJ_Nfyt77TEd2D9HlFP2zW3Lrgj8u5xFA3AJkJ3Rxbkk5BTf7SFURf_HFZh0jsvjDW8YlVLH2BdqL6wc3_yv2cYs2WUMhmVjLYmBLysWeJljccixatUE49oWJc4RWxAv5J3p2K3KRcuOPdi0fTVEB6jq6QkhUD2IYiRmBrEmUn4/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h19m04s413.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhqI9Ovi4rYpx_MzlAVvJ_Nfyt77TEd2D9HlFP2zW3Lrgj8u5xFA3AJkJ3Rxbkk5BTf7SFURf_HFZh0jsvjDW8YlVLH2BdqL6wc3_yv2cYs2WUMhmVjLYmBLysWeJljccixatUE49oWJc4RWxAv5J3p2K3KRcuOPdi0fTVEB6jq6QkhUD2IYiRmBrEmUn4/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h19m04s413.png.jpg)
It's totally personally to me!
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEitqDjbmnwemhiKSreHt4LzYRdPXFmz25Hk_ZmBVR3P85eA1qQVKXzuH1tQ_ZjydgSG9ITTiFRnZS36yIa758616XbH4IOYHHnk1itR4aY1ValDcfw-UmPCfUPJnV_TQxxRY_n6OrgcnDc5jKsCQZs1r7c7dLu6TWaKx-lSYzTqFe5wAD9XaNDQqy0UqKQ/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h26m41s097.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEitqDjbmnwemhiKSreHt4LzYRdPXFmz25Hk_ZmBVR3P85eA1qQVKXzuH1tQ_ZjydgSG9ITTiFRnZS36yIa758616XbH4IOYHHnk1itR4aY1ValDcfw-UmPCfUPJnV_TQxxRY_n6OrgcnDc5jKsCQZs1r7c7dLu6TWaKx-lSYzTqFe5wAD9XaNDQqy0UqKQ/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-05-21h26m41s097.png.jpg)
Squee! Okay. I'll save it forever.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgCDtkQ9Mwd9u4dBYRWriqVc794kqNC1F6XxAo2r-2EmszByad3QX1N_N4OUX5oIV3O-ygDbZxvab-axAoeg6siLys871JNoTtVvbh6o0OtvIiA0NNYLvgMWXhH3kNocsgINup9l1mCzuLqUun_BXMqbT3Zq_4OlCExLAGuA115SKcd5HDbXUUOhKHDtZQ/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-20h52m38s686.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgCDtkQ9Mwd9u4dBYRWriqVc794kqNC1F6XxAo2r-2EmszByad3QX1N_N4OUX5oIV3O-ygDbZxvab-axAoeg6siLys871JNoTtVvbh6o0OtvIiA0NNYLvgMWXhH3kNocsgINup9l1mCzuLqUun_BXMqbT3Zq_4OlCExLAGuA115SKcd5HDbXUUOhKHDtZQ/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-20h52m38s686.png.jpg)
Now, the next thing you would ordinarily do after an installation is pull down some binary packages to install the extra software you want. While OpenBSD's built-in `ftp` command is actually a pretty serviceable HTTPS client as well, `curl` is always a handy Swiss army tool to have around. The problem is, there aren't OpenBSD binary packages for `mips64el` (in fairness, this is also the case for Alpha, Luna 88K and PA-RISC). There is for _big-endian_ `mips64` like you'd run on an SGI, and this makes my grizzled old hacker heart glow, but not for this little black monster.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgKBqDx8b7dhe4BbsC2CNXmYA7o5IHg6mc91rbBnoJVr_NFBM_JmtKRUp-ixyEV3tSTT9WcTf6ba-kS8O0kJMIw0MNiMZsx5mWSzX30pkUKgpa0nQkpS7m4MpumR9EiDnhb76iYX6cIJWgcZ2_-oY9H3CnkXIDW28oWlfjMYLOFYeOFKrvUO1IL1CMbPg8/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-21h29m52s140.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgKBqDx8b7dhe4BbsC2CNXmYA7o5IHg6mc91rbBnoJVr_NFBM_JmtKRUp-ixyEV3tSTT9WcTf6ba-kS8O0kJMIw0MNiMZsx5mWSzX30pkUKgpa0nQkpS7m4MpumR9EiDnhb76iYX6cIJWgcZ2_-oY9H3CnkXIDW28oWlfjMYLOFYeOFKrvUO1IL1CMbPg8/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-06-21h29m52s140.png.jpg)
We'll have to build them from source. For this I added myself to the `wsrc` group, created the necessary directories in `/usr`, and then pulled down [the ports tree](https://www.openbsd.org/faq/ports/ports.html) over CVS.
Unfortunately OpenBSD's console driver for the Loongson systems does not seem to support multiple TTYs, so I put it in the background and pulled down the kernel source on another process. Pulling down the source and ports trees from CVS took the better part of a day and some of the night, but at least we now have metadata.
While I was at it, I turned off the additional startup reordering ( `# rcctl disable library_aslr`), noticeably speeding up reboots, and put myself as allowed to execute `rcctl` in `/etc/doas.conf`. See, I _can_ do things the OpenBSD-ish way when I _want_ to.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhP7Zzn92xvJ05shLk_nAWwPkrkh9cJ01YH_z5Zlj7KsOPJXK3UjQLl0Yww-skLicGtrpL0SsjYR_YlxwVnP1uE5Dhe2hN_Z6F-7YvkNJpZjIB6qOjNdeIYzsaHc2xfnntPx7NlRksDAAMJ6obu3higti5CbclP-7x5NcGM67bn9mXKC_YMxbsh5iGeiAA/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-20h12m31s982.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhP7Zzn92xvJ05shLk_nAWwPkrkh9cJ01YH_z5Zlj7KsOPJXK3UjQLl0Yww-skLicGtrpL0SsjYR_YlxwVnP1uE5Dhe2hN_Z6F-7YvkNJpZjIB6qOjNdeIYzsaHc2xfnntPx7NlRksDAAMJ6obu3higti5CbclP-7x5NcGM67bn9mXKC_YMxbsh5iGeiAA/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-20h12m31s982.png.jpg)
With the kernel source downloaded, so that we can eventually have the machine autoboot OpenBSD in the normal state, we'll create a new configuration hardcoding `sd0` as the root device ( `config bsd root on sd0a swap on sd0b dumps on sd0b`).
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjhyphenhyphenV_a6ju3cJk7qiQihyphenhyphen-ITnq8gz6pQ_435c73slfSLqNK_Qb23FDtYc0h323hTfjDRnu4dHShBxY7mM5UiLf72qnZxSOLKbtR-hhKB29JYQVsVHt0tPgnUm4bhYyNzOctJK-VYqdRJzCoNjinvMObev8LV7-chU0SyPQdCva-bn1OUSjhtsjoZIvekQA/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h38m18s018.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjhyphenhyphenV_a6ju3cJk7qiQihyphenhyphen-ITnq8gz6pQ_435c73slfSLqNK_Qb23FDtYc0h323hTfjDRnu4dHShBxY7mM5UiLf72qnZxSOLKbtR-hhKB29JYQVsVHt0tPgnUm4bhYyNzOctJK-VYqdRJzCoNjinvMObev8LV7-chU0SyPQdCva-bn1OUSjhtsjoZIvekQA/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h38m18s018.png.jpg)
The kernel build took about two hours and 15 minutes on the wall clock, after which I copied the new BSD kernel directly over to the Linux boot partition. This is _not_ the proper way to install a kernel on OpenBSD because you also need to install the pieces to reorder it, which is mandatory and `rcctl disable library_aslr` does not turn off. `make install` will do this for you, but I just wanted to see if it would work first.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEik54A0cemOoAXqIvF-JpbseUaC1QNq0E5SmmGxqhRjpNeb0cds8MAw4_gBFDmlmSEZ80Piq-aNWFz8TtAkxtG8hmbMkX0-ni5G_OKYpkC2oTE6ec4EMWd8C_NvB4-FkEtLcobchaj7tmVUWhpx9ZRipnHtxWCYrQ6ZlMpHGvm7dPRgZY9THEK8srrRYMs/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h39m20s688.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEik54A0cemOoAXqIvF-JpbseUaC1QNq0E5SmmGxqhRjpNeb0cds8MAw4_gBFDmlmSEZ80Piq-aNWFz8TtAkxtG8hmbMkX0-ni5G_OKYpkC2oTE6ec4EMWd8C_NvB4-FkEtLcobchaj7tmVUWhpx9ZRipnHtxWCYrQ6ZlMpHGvm7dPRgZY9THEK8srrRYMs/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h39m20s688.png.jpg)
And, verily, it did have the correct root device ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjYa5Liqv6Mk9ROtkmIWrT0UypF4shKCCbFg_Au5oiDh734QifvISHrmiwySqAYW_stuuiYZ62wAGFh7xt1HmkYbnO1E6gl54JUbq-1M5YDnwABfFEu63LMsY5hDuCyDBmTYiMZQQYz947S7jbv3s1CnCej_AfWDVlQu4CtC1eryJ1IvBpBwjUx-I-OaEY/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h40m07s757.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjYa5Liqv6Mk9ROtkmIWrT0UypF4shKCCbFg_Au5oiDh734QifvISHrmiwySqAYW_stuuiYZ62wAGFh7xt1HmkYbnO1E6gl54JUbq-1M5YDnwABfFEu63LMsY5hDuCyDBmTYiMZQQYz947S7jbv3s1CnCej_AfWDVlQu4CtC1eryJ1IvBpBwjUx-I-OaEY/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-07-22h40m07s757.png.jpg)
... but independent of the error that the kernel could not be reordered, which I expected, there was another odd error: `ldconfig: pledge: Invalid argument`
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEizdmODuhahvBb-8CzAdE9b-XjRUc5rWj8M3IP4BD-JajtI9mB0M2UJM4p4aQX8WmVpD3I6k6PIOvKg0Jt73_d_KlQfn3yMCI50wUVmY2mraH19zTc6YdkRb2_8bj9iVasEh1BWoI0oduMi_g3uXNXxhfUbwuT8tj_hpTLWCj5GdaSQQVGP-1vbw-pHZLQ/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h09m11s161.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEizdmODuhahvBb-8CzAdE9b-XjRUc5rWj8M3IP4BD-JajtI9mB0M2UJM4p4aQX8WmVpD3I6k6PIOvKg0Jt73_d_KlQfn3yMCI50wUVmY2mraH19zTc6YdkRb2_8bj9iVasEh1BWoI0oduMi_g3uXNXxhfUbwuT8tj_hpTLWCj5GdaSQQVGP-1vbw-pHZLQ/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h09m11s161.png.jpg)
In fact, this error was occurring all over the place. [`pledge(2)`](https://man.openbsd.org/pledge.2) is a way for a process to declare what subsystems or features it needs in advance, such that if Something Evil causes it to request a new one, the process will be righteously terminated into slag. A process can `pledge()` again and reduce its Christmas list further, but it can never increase it.
This is a core part of modern OpenBSD (introduced in 5.9) and lots of parts of the operating system use it now, so something significant had to have gone wrong with the new kernel. I contacted Miod Vallat directly, explained my predicament and invoked our good old days as posters on Nekochan, and he answered me anyway. More to the point, he was able to confirm that the stock 7.8 kernel worked, but not a newly built one off the same stable tree I was using.
After some additional investigation he found the answer in [the OpenBSD errata](https://www.openbsd.org/errata78.html), specifically [#18](https://ftp.openbsd.org/pub/OpenBSD/patches/7.8/common/018_pledgepaths.patch.sig). This tightens up the `pledge(2)` call to remove the `tmppath` promise, originally for the [`mkstemp(3)`](https://man.openbsd.org/mkstemp.3) family of functions, now obsolete because (in Theo's words) " [it sucks](https://undeadly.org/cgi?action=article;sid=20260226164038)" and had been completely superseded by [`unveil(2)`](https://man.openbsd.org/unveil.2) for requesting parts of a restricted filesystem view (introduced in 6.4). The utilities that were generating this error had already been patched in [earlier](https://ftp.openbsd.org/pub/OpenBSD/patches/7.8/common/015_tmppath.patch.sig) [errata](https://ftp.openbsd.org/pub/OpenBSD/patches/7.8/common/017_tmppath.patch.sig).
We already know we don't have binary packages and that also meant we didn't have binary patches, and _that_ also meant I'd have to rebuild those portions of the userland too, which didn't sound like an appetising prospect (and might have other dependencies). Or, Miod suggested, I could just wait for 7.9, which was about a week away.
I decided to wait for 7.9.

On the day of release (May 19), I grabbed an installer kernel and got cracking immediately to minimize the possibility one of those "sorry about that" errata would land again. Keeping the old 7.8 installer, I copied it to the Linux SSD ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiz6BbzFTdZ0dIvSjm6EKSqsIPKwJmddcp8fxG6MoFjKZmNr37Y15dBmLFVCjkI26cwPRXom3svSXZuLYyhsq3gvDFj3CNMF4_LpCdgW6WqIhlJvTSN8H-z7OSrQvJtJ4j6dpApEpVlMuTOlQUF_obxVgJYjXRUSkE8g48kvkumdQCPRvKJ-lE4v84EYbI/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h12m47s863.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiz6BbzFTdZ0dIvSjm6EKSqsIPKwJmddcp8fxG6MoFjKZmNr37Y15dBmLFVCjkI26cwPRXom3svSXZuLYyhsq3gvDFj3CNMF4_LpCdgW6WqIhlJvTSN8H-z7OSrQvJtJ4j6dpApEpVlMuTOlQUF_obxVgJYjXRUSkE8g48kvkumdQCPRvKJ-lE4v84EYbI/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h12m47s863.png.jpg)
... and started it. Here are the initial messages.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEikLjPwPeh9yEiwPtGeEFFVMLn_DWcGMrA9jtzPOoGWUp0foSMdf0PbMx0SEh7IonmHQ4rhDT-tWrLUP3EiVzHviPz4F4HXFnuruElOsShBDxRlvidFVE1hCLZD8pMkNTwkl-t24qKHQg1b0ZXG7aJ2fPsxH-yzGD6Ek0Zzxc8PqFBOUOV9zxBJF6rO0kw/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h15m00s731.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEikLjPwPeh9yEiwPtGeEFFVMLn_DWcGMrA9jtzPOoGWUp0foSMdf0PbMx0SEh7IonmHQ4rhDT-tWrLUP3EiVzHviPz4F4HXFnuruElOsShBDxRlvidFVE1hCLZD8pMkNTwkl-t24qKHQg1b0ZXG7aJ2fPsxH-yzGD6Ek0Zzxc8PqFBOUOV9zxBJF6rO0kw/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h15m00s731.png.jpg)
Actually, I figured this was a good exercise since now I'd have experience both doing a full install and then doing an in-place upgrade. On the upgrade path most of your settings, such as network interfaces, are read from your filesystem; you just have to tell it what that is.
I did try the `urtw0` adapter in the 7.9 installer and it crashed in the same way, so I should get off my butt and write a proper bug report.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj_Olkm6lVETdNDNoxzL8rk4Z1AnqL3hQfBFszstsv8b24fHbIlrbuu1qv0MCt_6EgJY1mIUC2_bg7U0tRERhnZSPvqaahrt8GTaFOujU7U6byDZFsVfUwKsE2lshrnOaWeErqu2OXV-wCJRwbNEDratSCfRZD2o6IbjoEpVK5OtOf8l3Zc5oUbbPNvgUU/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h31m05s754.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj_Olkm6lVETdNDNoxzL8rk4Z1AnqL3hQfBFszstsv8b24fHbIlrbuu1qv0MCt_6EgJY1mIUC2_bg7U0tRERhnZSPvqaahrt8GTaFOujU7U6byDZFsVfUwKsE2lshrnOaWeErqu2OXV-wCJRwbNEDratSCfRZD2o6IbjoEpVK5OtOf8l3Zc5oUbbPNvgUU/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h31m05s754.png.jpg)
Meanwhile, we then upgrade the sets (it remembered we were using Sonic) ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi-sxIuc6r6XmAH1tpbdZAHm3hyphenhyphenbwUJD0CPw2bBjlEDA7SUAJOvtlwr0M1TCa0ENXY1r5_GvW9nC9gkX77T8qNyTPQQddio9x9d-plWLyxgcIbswB97tXf5yvNSxv3yYCoV210VItya-qt4hMjhLrvLtBtIomwMnCZd_oOcJc2EpzUuvonyqumuNPAW2dw/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h58m26s500.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi-sxIuc6r6XmAH1tpbdZAHm3hyphenhyphenbwUJD0CPw2bBjlEDA7SUAJOvtlwr0M1TCa0ENXY1r5_GvW9nC9gkX77T8qNyTPQQddio9x9d-plWLyxgcIbswB97tXf5yvNSxv3yYCoV210VItya-qt4hMjhLrvLtBtIomwMnCZd_oOcJc2EpzUuvonyqumuNPAW2dw/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-18h58m26s500.png.jpg)

[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiBruq63JSxrDgkz6sKsuN1jvviNKwCEj1Q4TFuOcd5ujE-uBgTAXnPBicvFn6QZ8GMhjA4pvWwf6Q7_IGGwuT_8iAxGq2JPKFOD9Pn_KQZi931Ecgt0v53It_jgpBT_D30aD6FHbGctUQh3xlooWXJmgnCpzIDugG5f6vVT97pzAvAOqNHFNViljNn_A8/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-19h06m48s997.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiBruq63JSxrDgkz6sKsuN1jvviNKwCEj1Q4TFuOcd5ujE-uBgTAXnPBicvFn6QZ8GMhjA4pvWwf6Q7_IGGwuT_8iAxGq2JPKFOD9Pn_KQZi931Ecgt0v53It_jgpBT_D30aD6FHbGctUQh3xlooWXJmgnCpzIDugG5f6vVT97pzAvAOqNHFNViljNn_A8/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-19-19h06m48s997.png.jpg)
And that's it. The Linux "boot" partition on the SD card is also invisibly updated ...

Built with `make` ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi4yq5BcZxofFDNN81xVLqCuP8Yt8jTAaW6CXoakRrlshQb6XDZXhymsCoHobGvF9r9tZvu4X95sFnxym5Z9KrhvHXZ8q9dvyAGxC3Gko38aTzK7iw6BTJrU_p34DKqAM-DOViYk_qZGbNvGl1XQozAVt1BpXkUc1Nm9KFmagbf5ClIrTwMgyavN0R0hsM/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h18m32s033.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi4yq5BcZxofFDNN81xVLqCuP8Yt8jTAaW6CXoakRrlshQb6XDZXhymsCoHobGvF9r9tZvu4X95sFnxym5Z9KrhvHXZ8q9dvyAGxC3Gko38aTzK7iw6BTJrU_p34DKqAM-DOViYk_qZGbNvGl1XQozAVt1BpXkUc1Nm9KFmagbf5ClIrTwMgyavN0R0hsM/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h18m32s033.png.jpg)
... and installed as root with `make install`. Now we have a proper shell.
Since I considered the system minimally-meaningfully configured at this point, I wanted to see if I could duplicate the same performance results on OpenBSD. The result ... wasn't the same. Remember how I mentioned something about "the worst part" before? Here's the worst part.
``` 2K performance run parameters for coremark. CoreMark Size : 666 Total ticks : 15649 Total time (secs): 15.649000 Iterations/Sec : 1278.036935 Iterations : 20000 Compiler version : GCCOpenBSD Clang 19.1.7 Compiler flags : -O3 -DPERFORMANCE_RUN=1
```
That's surprisingly bad, about 30% slower. Although Clang doesn't appear to support any special optimizations for Loongson, that certainly wouldn't explain the entire delta. CoreMark shouldn't be significantly affected by system call performance or kernel issues, so that leaves the compiler's code quality. My working theory is Clang just doesn't generate good code for Loongson compared to `gcc` (I'll defer to others if it does for MIPS64 generally), and we're going to see the consequences of that shortly.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjgdf61W27oDfCibZcetMtIcTv1D7ESTgHKF8uFy37L57k6rWGePSnVRahL7OzmBTJE2xIoGTmQMI7SREknwE1cPYc2McF9Bm78aaUji0G67j02RRWEdqmas-GNm0HXgX3_Lzi6P_JBCWPUHdD0bykJ8C2W6xvbUxMdXUfX6tREQWy_FpN2eG8e_FdAIGI/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h40m21s399.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjgdf61W27oDfCibZcetMtIcTv1D7ESTgHKF8uFy37L57k6rWGePSnVRahL7OzmBTJE2xIoGTmQMI7SREknwE1cPYc2McF9Bm78aaUji0G67j02RRWEdqmas-GNm0HXgX3_Lzi6P_JBCWPUHdD0bykJ8C2W6xvbUxMdXUfX6tREQWy_FpN2eG8e_FdAIGI/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h40m21s399.png.jpg)
Let's also see how it functions in X (Wayland? ha ha ha). OpenBSD's flavour of Xorg 7.7 — they're clear it's not a fork — is called [Xenocara](https://www.xenocara.org/). I like my client systems to boot as text and then to start the window system as a second phase in case something goes awry with the graphics hardware. In the Yeeloong's case the text display is apparently also being run through the framebuffer but I'm going to run this machine the same way. We can manually start Xenocara's display manager ( `xenodm`) with `rcctl -f start xenodm` from a root shell, though I forgot I already gave myself `rcctl` permissions in `doas.conf`.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnS7tPy63hFpz8aptJtTqwQ8fokrblczd6lSleoXTZJub9ngnNnERh5fZfLMt2Tq0VU33L1bAJNuPkFujfPV6ZVYxuMZQL7LM5N2UpvqxihbDUV142YdCHwE0vLR-ILUqIoH04sEciTo-mauDjdk4MDgtwIts84Lnpst5SjS2YW3BePr1pS99IFPw4TIo/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h40m44s799.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnS7tPy63hFpz8aptJtTqwQ8fokrblczd6lSleoXTZJub9ngnNnERh5fZfLMt2Tq0VU33L1bAJNuPkFujfPV6ZVYxuMZQL7LM5N2UpvqxihbDUV142YdCHwE0vLR-ILUqIoH04sEciTo-mauDjdk4MDgtwIts84Lnpst5SjS2YW3BePr1pS99IFPw4TIo/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-20-19h40m44s799.png.jpg)
This requires us to log in anew. Now that we have X up, we will use `xwd` for direct pixel perfect grabs while we're there. There is no special acceleration for the SiliconMotion SM712 or the SIMD features of the Loongson-2F.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhdVpmVPgXavDbw9WaLnU1NV6tYfXW1yy3BwrcoQOIwlxAqCrHtuf5_n3e2xw_8hvjYtdXv_H8G5hoVwWURqYlymOFh4AG95HMBOFzD8cylzqLL3cYH1ZqLWGKyszXuTjAjCiAzvX9fCBNZht1Cq1layYzlT3ZiTQVj-nqbnSn0gfGvCTevexW5XxXLJQA/s320/000013.xwd.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhdVpmVPgXavDbw9WaLnU1NV6tYfXW1yy3BwrcoQOIwlxAqCrHtuf5_n3e2xw_8hvjYtdXv_H8G5hoVwWURqYlymOFh4AG95HMBOFzD8cylzqLL3cYH1ZqLWGKyszXuTjAjCiAzvX9fCBNZht1Cq1layYzlT3ZiTQVj-nqbnSn0gfGvCTevexW5XxXLJQA/s2048/000013.xwd.png)
By default OpenBSD provides [`fvwm`](https://www.fvwm.org/), the F? Virtual Window Manager where F can stand for anything you like, long ago forked (one example of an F word) from the venerable `twm`. Its primary claim to fame is [its highly configurable nature](https://www.fvwm.org/Wiki/Config/Fvwm2rc/) which I will doubtlessly hack on, but this is what the system provides you to start with. With no `~/.xsession`, you get vanilla `fvwm` and an `xterm`, and maybe a leftover `xconsole` from the display manager. Nine workspaces are selectable from the overview in the lower right corner.
The trackpad is as bad in OpenBSD as it is in Linux, which more likely means the trackpad sucks no matter who's trying to read it. The most irritating thing is a lot of phantom pointer motion which is really obnoxious when focus follows the pointer as it does here. My fat fingers also frequently seemed to turn what I merely intended as motion into unexpected drags.
Left click opens the root menu. There is only one standard application pre-installed in it, namely `xterm`.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiJnJEl0YQBQIIV42cyxcfrpIT-NdOM3vHMoyMMPjjZDEOrBUQT726US3VvExakwPbx-oA9pcABvcsis1ZedW0Wjty1tmJrfw9rmz0VO6xbp7ggUz2in4R5DHlymoRH-oCrLGhwO9GCuqSQoVYUQ35jLEvLA75heFB7psGqQOrJSW7XttA0HjguF25RFPA/s320/000011.xwd.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiJnJEl0YQBQIIV42cyxcfrpIT-NdOM3vHMoyMMPjjZDEOrBUQT726US3VvExakwPbx-oA9pcABvcsis1ZedW0Wjty1tmJrfw9rmz0VO6xbp7ggUz2in4R5DHlymoRH-oCrLGhwO9GCuqSQoVYUQ35jLEvLA75heFB7psGqQOrJSW7XttA0HjguF25RFPA/s2048/000011.xwd.png)
Right click opens the list of windows, though the console window doesn't appear in this list even when open.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjUXVj5GjB5zAlAKj2bmEx_rU58A0BzRiPG3tbpas5d0SRiL2yYJr96INCUFe7AzyHCSqoBjx7PZjPkH-HpIkPEM7ajlUnmYweqZLskQycMx3oWZr96VkeKMP2NBAThzb1pW9VgJBVuAA1nwVI0qZ9FT6WZCCeiNRNXOhgvpfdJVd2NGD21X1odDacdYaE/s320/000003.xwd.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjUXVj5GjB5zAlAKj2bmEx_rU58A0BzRiPG3tbpas5d0SRiL2yYJr96INCUFe7AzyHCSqoBjx7PZjPkH-HpIkPEM7ajlUnmYweqZLskQycMx3oWZr96VkeKMP2NBAThzb1pW9VgJBVuAA1nwVI0qZ9FT6WZCCeiNRNXOhgvpfdJVd2NGD21X1odDacdYaE/s2048/000003.xwd.png)
Left click in the window's close gadget opens the window-specific menu with the usual options such as moving, changing stacking order, iconifying or killing outright.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhRV1e8Rmb-UMkqPGHh6ZUVXiP1l5eeTQQ3pAhqhnFdrrTVpkDqco8umBvf_b_eW0PWcRS0Ry_2iVrtS_P6L5YkBwNj8DIGfT7I62j_Z2ORcRj3717smRQAQm2RGdlboZpsiVF_gdfVLbTVSNUqkOqzB1Z71o936Iugs7yChumjxvFBtxCa00txnfc1OyA/s320/000012.xwd.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhRV1e8Rmb-UMkqPGHh6ZUVXiP1l5eeTQQ3pAhqhnFdrrTVpkDqco8umBvf_b_eW0PWcRS0Ry_2iVrtS_P6L5YkBwNj8DIGfT7I62j_Z2ORcRj3717smRQAQm2RGdlboZpsiVF_gdfVLbTVSNUqkOqzB1Z71o936Iugs7yChumjxvFBtxCa00txnfc1OyA/s2048/000012.xwd.png)
Alternatively, you can go verb-initial and pick an action by "middle click" (both buttons), then pick a window to apply it to.
There is also a scroll strip on the trackpad abutting the right button that basically functions like a set of scroll up and scroll down buttons. Once you understand this, it works largely like you'd expect as long as you're verrrry deliberate about where your finger is.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiFsv3y0bqQn9Rnw3VUuO8INxL-XjZ_xvsJrAl-fnSAyz3lhj37KnoDDX_0YurvQLxUWiK7Mtjrewu_itaBOlrzGYaQ4pDAJrc1-K-G1MeJF2yQH8yXdnqLBHtYGiBVLc_iqfsD0VGDsGVyynAKwx7ZttjA9K5G2CDRefpP2ROvypGE3U5tPVKCPSZh21w/s320/000014.xwd.png)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiFsv3y0bqQn9Rnw3VUuO8INxL-XjZ_xvsJrAl-fnSAyz3lhj37KnoDDX_0YurvQLxUWiK7Mtjrewu_itaBOlrzGYaQ4pDAJrc1-K-G1MeJF2yQH8yXdnqLBHtYGiBVLc_iqfsD0VGDsGVyynAKwx7ZttjA9K5G2CDRefpP2ROvypGE3U5tPVKCPSZh21w/s2048/000014.xwd.png)
At this point you could simply quit, or even (a little more pointedly) `rcctl stop xenodm` (as root, or from `doas`).
I like this way of launching X11, even more so than the usual `startx`, so I made an alias: `alias x exec doas rcctl -f start xenodm`
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjl_TD00PLPoBx6QomkrY4C77SIaQMqN2zq18kyRyWUGmEUjtfi__yx8SBzGUCl08y2GFVIWqE9o2o7a3UcGWbfe4YNJLqKcWMBhMPxrd7LJ_xInyfyKPPova3nWpB3BAFrADQp-zmlfBqYGetTa8Gy1E2py-OhwczZ6EiCspACdEReXFv6CPwgXhyphenhyphenQc9w/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-18h28m27s752.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjl_TD00PLPoBx6QomkrY4C77SIaQMqN2zq18kyRyWUGmEUjtfi__yx8SBzGUCl08y2GFVIWqE9o2o7a3UcGWbfe4YNJLqKcWMBhMPxrd7LJ_xInyfyKPPova3nWpB3BAFrADQp-zmlfBqYGetTa8Gy1E2py-OhwczZ6EiCspACdEReXFv6CPwgXhyphenhyphenQc9w/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-18h28m27s752.png.jpg)
The last step of our basic customization is to do something about that big honking console font. The Loongson port doesn't seem to have much, if any, `wscons` support; there is no scrollback and `wsconscfg` gives me an error when I try to add a screen. That leaves (you guessed it) hardcoding the font choice in the kernel configuration, so I added `FONT_SPLEEN8x16` to the `options` and rebuilt it. Now we have the same smaller font as the installer kernel.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh9nicECjk1DS7oiMNGmR2rbtXAAazPNTDh3H6UWXwMgfee5TPTnCdoXQkv_irXtWy4WsEab390EaXAiFXWhyMg8ZramqXplVQWmt2j0_8L0gLOcTS6z9rO4gsDCSSmPGdBJ-kyFcTpBLcAJAkCa-5gdj0x3MaTYdv66a-_7GmoBpwkOlo4O_MgXc-ISiA/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-18h50m52s174.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh9nicECjk1DS7oiMNGmR2rbtXAAazPNTDh3H6UWXwMgfee5TPTnCdoXQkv_irXtWy4WsEab390EaXAiFXWhyMg8ZramqXplVQWmt2j0_8L0gLOcTS6z9rO4gsDCSSmPGdBJ-kyFcTpBLcAJAkCa-5gdj0x3MaTYdv66a-_7GmoBpwkOlo4O_MgXc-ISiA/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-18h50m52s174.png.jpg)
We have our kernel the way we want it, but we still have to manually boot it by breaking out of the boot sequence, so we should rectify that as well. Lemote PMON does have some basic provision for a boot menu, stored in `(wd0,0)/boot/boot.cfg`. The previous owner had disabled this by setting the `ShowBootMenu` variable in PMON to `no`, so we'll start by writing up a simple menu file. We'll offer three options, namely booting OpenBSD off the SD card using our customized kernel on the internal storage (the default), booting Debian 6 on the internal storage, or booting an OpenBSD installer kernel on the internal storage. Our file looks like this:
``` timeout 5 default 0 showmenu 1
title OpenBSD on SD kernel (wd0,0)/bsd args --
title Debian 6 kernel (wd0,0)/boot/vmlinux args console=tty root=/dev/sda1 quiet
title OpenBSD on RD kernel (wd0,0)/bsd.rd args --
```
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgRsn7_6Ul3tnZpReAnkhPWLnN3gwyupf0HeAlNNyacnT-RsbMtNY3dlBZCgxBS_ddEqkw6CtSIjnFFPzRwJQ0B9LnQ85HjqsDKuh-B334qbdEPU5MhL_2XS3ojPfupYyFsa7r-UpmnNVgdrvDMPd1U8FXTvjhodCTh7qkP3AZZfEhLU8OCdRBaJsQgQqE/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h06m51s856.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgRsn7_6Ul3tnZpReAnkhPWLnN3gwyupf0HeAlNNyacnT-RsbMtNY3dlBZCgxBS_ddEqkw6CtSIjnFFPzRwJQ0B9LnQ85HjqsDKuh-B334qbdEPU5MhL_2XS3ojPfupYyFsa7r-UpmnNVgdrvDMPd1U8FXTvjhodCTh7qkP3AZZfEhLU8OCdRBaJsQgQqE/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h06m51s856.png.jpg)
Finally, `set ShowBootMenu yes` in PMON sets the new default in flash.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhys8Fz2wQwbLSQO7MQdFgew21QzZQsTN6VAegFWmFR19uocOZoJ4hxQ9ZgiibLC-yW4Coe-TKZYvCOmz7hyphenhyphenCodk-Z6impVQKM0PHWefGvOqyfef7OWnJeh6w_KLdk_z4g_NFxBMZkuU-MZZIqATOJR4NrrDQvlwsf0Lix_Gj0bUR_ML67IXk8w6vZD3QI/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h07m08s743.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhys8Fz2wQwbLSQO7MQdFgew21QzZQsTN6VAegFWmFR19uocOZoJ4hxQ9ZgiibLC-yW4Coe-TKZYvCOmz7hyphenhyphenCodk-Z6impVQKM0PHWefGvOqyfef7OWnJeh6w_KLdk_z4g_NFxBMZkuU-MZZIqATOJR4NrrDQvlwsf0Lix_Gj0bUR_ML67IXk8w6vZD3QI/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h07m08s743.png.jpg)
On the next reboot (or `reboot` from PMON itself), our menu automatically appears, and option 0 is selected after five seconds, and our kernel boots. We also have an easy way of getting to PMON directly here instead of having to mash Delete.
But what took most of the time after that was when I "just" decided to install a web browser, and I even chose one I thought was small: [NetSurf](https://www.netsurf-browser.org/). That's a graphical browser lightweight enough that it should let me know if installing a heavier browser would be at all feasible on this hardware, especially given the disappointing CoreMark score. Here is the point at which I complain a little about the taste of the free beer, and for that I apologise (somewhat), so fair warning is hereby given.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhWk307_N3IeRB2-1XtD8p7tq10dA4NrUuHOst-A6iFIZ8IrQIluACldeZSIQulCeRMkj6HfwHWvjrIHpKeSdWHGv3ovsyekq9vPely8e7U4YDTBEUwMi_NiuvqzIm8Re1BbeigIKrmsAAezHGX-AEd2biUhG5gIXotUaEcAy9bUWohjG_nQPyqmeRSA24/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h35m32s235.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhWk307_N3IeRB2-1XtD8p7tq10dA4NrUuHOst-A6iFIZ8IrQIluACldeZSIQulCeRMkj6HfwHWvjrIHpKeSdWHGv3ovsyekq9vPely8e7U4YDTBEUwMi_NiuvqzIm8Re1BbeigIKrmsAAezHGX-AEd2biUhG5gIXotUaEcAy9bUWohjG_nQPyqmeRSA24/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-21-19h35m32s235.png.jpg)
Perhaps I went about it the wrong way, but I tried very hard not to build the whole thing as root. I knew there would be dependencies and I thought that the build system would be smart enough to use `doas` or something to properly install the packages for me, and it turns out I thought wrong. Maybe I should figure out [`dpb`](https://man.openbsd.org/dpb) if I'm going to do this for any non-trivial package, or set something up to cross-compile, but none of those things seemed obviously necessary when I embarked on the whole affair. The magic spell was `doas pkg_add -D unsigned /usr/ports/packages/mips64el/all/package.tgz` and this would (after spuriously checking Sonic for a binary even though I just gave it one as a command line argument, which also seems like a bug) force-install the package in question.
Making it worse was the fact that all the build system requirements that wouldn't ordinarily be required for runtime (e.g., `meson`, `ninja`, CMake) had to be compiled too, which I'm calling the "build-from-source tax." It _sucks_ to pay the build-from-source tax on an underspec'ed platform. In fairness the ports system does at least use a partial GNU `configure` cache between package builds, but this didn't seem to be the case with `meson` or CMake, although they also checked for fewer things.
All of these factors caused the build time for NetSurf to balloon to _two weeks_ in a herky jerky fashion where I left it to run while I was at work, or overnight, and pushed a few packages through, let it run again, came back and pushed some more, and so on, and so on, and so on. Someone tell me what I did wrong here. It feels like it shouldn't have been this arduous.
That said, I'd also like to add how amazed I was at the Yeeloong's reliability, not something I was expecting _at all_ (especially considering the horror stories around the EMTEC Gdium). I didn't ever have to power it off or reboot it — that poor fan was going full kilter by the end, and the build times were agony, but it never bugged out or locked up throughout the entire ordeal. Maybe this one secretly has a Loongson-2G in it.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhvmhQxQ1AQY49FFZC9WCPOMj48Ffsu4iRnp1C1RGeHYKh00ShSYMr2n7nNcJXXS4tbfzxMKL8FRnUe0X-Z7LU-DFKFeFgZGFh-M7ovlsXkRlg3AktA9QK57vEVojR70KmEQzInUz2AdrRjjv0_9SF63fBu86dk9keibidBBvUdJUbBW0Y-uBAxKFJN-SA/s320/vlcsnap-2026-05-25-18h56m57s897.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhvmhQxQ1AQY49FFZC9WCPOMj48Ffsu4iRnp1C1RGeHYKh00ShSYMr2n7nNcJXXS4tbfzxMKL8FRnUe0X-Z7LU-DFKFeFgZGFh-M7ovlsXkRlg3AktA9QK57vEVojR70KmEQzInUz2AdrRjjv0_9SF63fBu86dk9keibidBBvUdJUbBW0Y-uBAxKFJN-SA/s2052/vlcsnap-2026-05-25-18h56m57s897.png.jpg)
I was also amused by how opinionated the linker in OpenBSD is about deprecated or (in its view) dangerous functions, which was a welcome bit of levity in an otherwise drudgerous exercise. Notice in that sea of compiler options that it's building at `-O2`. I'm going to come back to this point.
In the end it was absolutely freaking remarkable how _freaking everything_ is a freaking prerequisite for _freaking everything else_. For just NetSurf, `tcsh` and `xv`, these are all the packages it had to build:
